title: Heart Health. Grape Polyphenols Reduce Blood Pressure and Increase Flow-Mediated Vasodilation in Men with Metabolic Syndrome. In this study, men with metabolic syndrome consumed either grape powder or a placebo for 30 days then switched to the alternate diet for 30 days following a 3-week washout period. Adding grapes to the diet improved vascular function by promoting relaxation of the arteries, reducing blood pressure, and reducing certain inflammatory molecules.

sentences.text: Heart Health.
Grape Polyphenols Reduce Blood Pressure and Increase Flow-Mediated Vasodilation in Men with Metabolic Syndrome.
In this study, men with metabolic syndrome consumed either grape powder or a placebo for 30 days then switched to the alternate diet for 30 days following a 3-week washout period.
Adding grapes to the diet improved vascular function by promoting relaxation of the arteries, reducing blood pressure, and reducing certain inflammatory molecules.
Vasoprotective Endothelial Effects of a Standardized Grape Product in Humans.
In this human study, consuming grape powder in an amount equivalent to 1 1/4 cups of fresh grapes per day helped improve blood vessel function in five healthy adult male subjects.
Consuming the same amount of grape powder, but twice a day for 21 days, caused even greater improvements in blood vessel function and antioxidant capacity.
This study also demonstrated that consuming grapes with a high fat meal helped prevent the damaging impact, reduced blood flow, that was observed with a high fat meal consumed without grapes.
Grape Polyphenols Exert a Cardioprotective Effect in Pre- and Post-menopausal Women by Lowering Plasma Lipids and Reducing Oxidative Stress.
Key risk factors for coronary heart disease were reduced in pre- and post-menopausal women consuming the equivalent of 1 1/4 cups of grapes per day.
These included a reduction in blood triglyceride levels, L D L cholesterol levels, and key markers for inflammation in the body.
Additionally, the researchers observed a significant reduction in whole body oxidative stress.
Vascular and Cardiac Effects of Grape Powder in the Spontaneously Hypertensive Rat.
This study investigated the effects of grape consumption in spontaneously hypertensive rats.
No changes to the physiology of the heart were observed.
However, grape consumption elicited a significant reduction in blood pressure, improved arterial relaxation, increased vascular compliance, and attenuated cardiac hypertrophy.
Grapes Added to the Diet Helped Lower Blood Pressure, Improve Heart Function, Reduce Inflammation, and Reduce Heart Muscle Damage Associated with a High-Salt Diet.
In a series of laboratory animal studies, a grape-enriched diet helped protect against high blood pressure and the development of heart failure commonly associated with a high-salt diet.
One study examined the impact of adding grapes to the diet of animals consuming either a high- or low-salt diet and also those receiving a mild dose of a common blood pressure drug, hydrazine.

Those consuming a grape-enriched diet had lower blood pressure, better heart function, reduced inflammation throughout their bodies, and fewer signs of heart muscle damage than those consuming the same diet but without grapes.
The group receiving the blood pressure medicine, but no grapes, demonstrated a reduction in blood pressure, but their hearts were not protected from damage as was the case in the grape-fed group.
Two additional studies, using a similar study model and feeding a grape-enriched diet, also demonstrated these types of protective effects.
Grape Powder Polyphenols Attenuate Atherosclerosis Development in Apolipoprotein E-Deficient Mice and Reduce Macrophage Atherogenicity.
This study found that a grape-enriched diet helped prevent the accumulation of harmful oxidized cholesterol and reduced the development of atherosclerotic lesions by 41 percent.

The animals consuming the grape-enriched diet also exhibited reduced oxidative stress, increased serum antioxidant capacity, reduced cell uptake of oxidized cholesterol, and decreased oxidation of L D L cholesterol.
Grape Polyphenols Decrease Plasma Triglycerides and Cholesterol Accumulation in the Aorta of Ovariectomized Guinea Pigs.
In an animal model for menopausal women, female ovariectomized guinea pigs were fed either a control diet or a grape-enriched diet for 12 weeks.
Dietary cholesterol was added to both diets to raise plasma cholesterol levels and promote the development of atherosclerosis.

Study findings showed that while plasma L D L concentrations did not differ between the groups, those consuming the grape-enriched diet had 39 percent lower plasma triglycerides, 50 percent lower V L D L cholesterol, and 33 percent lower concentrations of cholesterol in the aorta, compared with animals on the control diet.
